important_freedom_fighter_quotes.quote: In a gentle way, you can shake the world.
A moment comes, which comes but rarely in history, when we step out from the old to the new.
Give me blood and I will give you freedom.
Swaraj is my birthright, and I shall have it.

module_test.mains_essay_1: Analyze the factors that enabled British expansion in India from 1600-1757

topics.topic_name: Early European Trading Companies & Portuguese Rule

primary_book.author: Bipin Chandra
primary_book.chapter_name: European Expansion in Asia and the Beginning of European Rule in India

ncert_reference.book_name: Our Pasts III
India and the Contemporary World II
ncert_reference.chapter_name: The Rise of Nationalism in Europe

key_concepts: Vasco da Gama arrival (1498) and circumnavigation of Africa
Portuguese dominance in Indian Ocean trade (1498-1740s)
Albuquerque's strategy: control of naval routes and fortified posts
Portuguese settlements: Goa (capital), Diu, Daman, and Cochin
Trade monopoly system and Portuguese economic policies
Spice trade significance for European economy
Decline of Portuguese due to competition from other European powers

Impact on Indian economy: drain of precious metals, disruption of existing trade
Portuguese cultural and religious influence in India
Arab and Venetian traders' role before European arrival
Monsoon winds and maritime geography importance
Religious missions and evangelization under Portuguese rule

key_personalities: Vasco da Gama: Portuguese explorer, first European sea route to India
Alfonso de Albuquerque: Portuguese Governor, strategist
King Manuel I: Portuguese monarch, patron of exploration
Zamorin of Calicut: Indian ruler resisting Portuguese monopoly
key_terms: Carreira da India: Portuguese spice fleet route
Cartaz system: Portuguese trading permit system
Monsoon winds: Seasonal weather patterns enabling sea travel

topics.topic_name: East India Company Establishment & Early Settlements

Company structure: Joint-stock company model, governance by Court of Directors

Comparison with Dutch, French, and Portuguese companies
Company's initial trading objectives: textiles, spices, indigo, cotton
Diplomatic relations with Mughal Empire and regional rulers
Company policies on local trade and indigenous merchants
Factory system: fortified trading settlements with warehouses
Evolution from merchant activity to military expansion
Conflict with existing Portuguese and Dutch traders
Administrative structure in settlements
Banking and financial systems development
Impact on local economy and merchant classes

key_dates: 1608: First British factory at Surat established
1661: Bombay received as dowry from Portugal
1698: East India Company purchased Calcutta from Nawab

Sir Josiah Child: Governor of EIC (1681-1690)
Emperor Shah Jahan: Mughal ruler during early EIC expansion
key_settlements: Bombay: Strategic location on west coast (1661)
Madras: Trading settlement on east coast (1639)

ncert_reference.book_name: Our Pasts III

key_concepts: Subordinate isolation policy: dividing Indian powers
Subsidiary alliances: military arrangements forcing dependence
Doctrine of Lapse: annexation of lapsed princely states (introduced 1848)
Strategic locations and geopolitical importance
Anglo-Maratha Wars (1775-1818): three decisive conflicts

Battle of Buxar (1764): consolidation in Bengal and Bihar
Mysore Wars (1767-1799): Tipu Sultan and British expansion
Anglo-Sikh Wars (1845-1849): Punjab conquest
Decline of Mughal Empire: fragmentation and weakness
Regional powers: Marathas, Mysore, Sikhs, Bengal nawabs
Use of native troops: sepoys and recruitment policies
Political divisions exploited by British
Technology advantage: artillery and naval superiority
Treaty-making as expansion tool

1764: Battle of Buxar (British victory in Bihar)

Siraj-ud-Daulah: Bengal nawab defeated at Plassey

Ranjit Singh: Sikh kingdom builder, resisted British
Baji Rao II: Maratha leader
Lord Cornwallis: Governor-General, administrative reforms
key_terms: Jagir system: feudal land grants disrupted
Zamindari: landlord system consolidated under British

module_test.mains_essay: Analyze the economic policies of British rule and their consequences for Indian economy and society

key_dates: 1700s: Indian textiles dominant in world trade
1813: East India Company monopoly partially ended
1850: Indian share of world GDP drops from 23% to 4%

British textile exports to India: multiplied 100x in 50 years
Indian manufacturing share: 25% of world (1800) to 2% (1900)
Unemployment in traditional industries: millions of artisans
key_industries_affected: Silk and wool: production declined drastically
Steel and iron: traditional production ceased

modules.module_name: Revolt of 1857 — First War of Independence
key_focus_areas: Causes and triggers of the Revolt
Spread and leadership of the Revolt

module_test.mains_essay_1: Examine the causes of the Revolt of 1857 and critically evaluate the extent to which it was a 'War of Independence'
module_test.mains_essay_2: Analyze the regional variations in the Revolt of 1857 and explain why it failed despite widespread participation

ncert_reference.book_name: Our Pasts III
ncert_reference.chapter_name: Weavers, Iron Smelters and Factory Owners

ncert_reference.book_name: India and the Contemporary World II

Religious sentiments: Christian missionary activities

Doctrine of Lapse: annexation of princely states
General Service Enlistment Act: overseas service compulsion

Intellectual ferment and nationalist ideas spreading

Local rulers' loss of power and prestige

Immediate trigger: Enfield cartridge controversy (January 1857)

key_dates: 1856: Enfield rifle introduction and cartridge controversy
May 10, 1857: Meerut uprising and Delhi march

Lapse Doctrine: annexation of Satara, Nagpur, Jhansi

Rani Lakshmibai: Jhansi resistance leader

participation_levels: Peasants: widespread in North-Western Provinces, Awadh

leadership_roles: Political leaders: Bahadur Shah Zafar, Nana Sahib
Military strategists: Tatya Tope, Kunwar Singh
Religious leaders: Maulvi Liaquat Ali, Ahmadullah
Women leaders: Rani Lakshmibai, Begum Hazrat Mahal

Nana Sahib: Maratha chief, lost title and income
Kunwar Singh: Zamindar of Jagdispur, elderly leader
Bahadur Shah Zafar: 82-year-old emperor, exiled to Burma

provisions: Stipulated East India Company's accountability to British Parliament

provisions: Dual control system: Crown and East India Company

provisions: Separation of executive and judicial functions
District collectors appointed as chief administrators

1 lakh rupees for Indian education allocation

provisions: Indian Civil Service established for British officers

provisions: Crown took direct control of India

provisions: Enhanced imperial status of Crown rule

study_tips_for_modern_history.chronological_mapping: Create timeline of events (1600-1947) with major turning points
study_tips_for_modern_history.connections: Link causes of 1857 to rise of nationalism and independence struggle
study_tips_for_modern_history.personalities: Associate leaders with their contributions and time periods
study_tips_for_modern_history.act_memorization: Remember acts with years, provisions, and significance
study_tips_for_modern_history.regional_focus: Understand regional variations in revolt and nationalist movements
study_tips_for_modern_history.economic_impact: Connect economic policies to peasant grievances and movements
study_tips_for_modern_history.quote_retention: Remember key quotes from leaders for mains answers
study_tips_for_modern_history.map_practice: Practice identifying regions of different movements and battles
